metadata.title: Adaptive Designs for Clinical Trials of Drugs and Biologics — Guidance for Industry

rules.rule_text: For an adaptive design to be acceptable, the chance of erroneous conclusions must be adequately controlled, estimation of treatment effects must be sufficiently reliable, design details must be completely prespecified, and trial integrity must be appropriately maintained.
rules.source_section: Section III — Principles for Adaptive Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: the design, conduct, and analysis of an adaptive clinical trial intended to provide substantial evidence of effectiveness should satisfy four key principles: the chance of erroneous conclusions should be adequately controlled, estimation of treatment effects should be sufficiently reliable, details of the design should be completely prespecified, and trial integrity should be appropriately maintained.

rules.rule_text: Adaptive design proposals for trials incorporating null hypothesis testing must address the possibility of Type I error probability inflation; appropriate methods must be used to control the Type I error probability at the desired level (typically 0.025 one-sided).
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Controlling the Chance of Erroneous Conclusions
rules.verbatim_quote: Adaptive design proposals for trials incorporating null hypothesis testing should therefore address the possibility of Type I error probability inflation. In some cases, such as simple group sequential designs (section V.A.), statistical theory can be used to derive significance levels that ensure Type I error probability is controlled at the desired level.
rules.exceptions: Non-comparative adaptive designs based on blinded pooled data generally have no or limited effect on Type I error probability

rules.rule_text: When methods for adjusting treatment effect estimates to reduce bias are available for a given adaptive design, those methods should be prospectively planned and used for reporting results.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Estimating Treatment Effects
rules.verbatim_quote: For some designs there are known methods for adjusting estimates to reduce or remove bias associated with adaptations and to improve performance on measures such as the mean squared error. Such methods should be prospectively planned and used for reporting results when they are available.
rules.exceptions: Where methods are not available, treatment effect estimates and confidence intervals should be presented with appropriate cautions regarding their interpretation

rules.rule_text: All details of the adaptive design must be completely specified prior to initiation of the trial and documented accordingly, including prespecification of the anticipated number and timing of interim analyses, the type of adaptation, the statistical inferential methods, and the specific algorithm governing the adaptation decision.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — Trial Planning
rules.verbatim_quote: it is expected that the details of the adaptive design are completely specified prior to initiation of the trial and documented accordingly (section VIII.B.). Prospective planning should include prespecification of the anticipated number and timing of interim analyses, the type of adaptation, the statistical inferential methods to be used, and the specific algorithm governing the adaptation decision.
rules.exceptions: Monitoring committee recommendations might occasionally deviate from the anticipated algorithm based on the totality of the data

rules.rule_text: Access to comparative interim results must be limited to individuals with relevant expertise who are independent of the personnel involved in conducting or managing the trial; patients, investigators, and sponsor personnel must not have access to comparative interim results.
rules.source_section: Section III.D — Maintaining Trial Conduct and Integrity
rules.verbatim_quote: for all clinical trials (adaptive and non-adaptive) it is strongly recommended that access to comparative interim results be limited to individuals with relevant expertise who are independent of the personnel involved in conducting or managing the trial.
rules.exceptions: Limited access for specific sponsor personnel can be justified for certain adaptive decisions (e.g., dose selection) under defined conditions

rules.rule_text: Sponsors are encouraged to discuss adaptive design considerations with the appropriate FDA review division at regulatory meetings such as End-of-Phase-2 (EOP2) or Type C meetings before initiation of late-phase adaptive trials.
rules.source_section: Section II.E — Choosing to Adapt
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA encourages sponsors to explore a variety of design options in planning and to discuss their considerations with the appropriate FDA review division at regulatory meetings such as End-of-Phase-2 (EOP2) or Type C meetings.

rules.rule_text: Adequately prespecified adaptations based on non-comparative (blinded/masked) data generally do not inflate Type I error probability and are acceptable when uncertainty about event probabilities or endpoint variability is high, but sponsors must evaluate the extent of inflation in non-inferiority or equivalence trials.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Adaptive Designs Based on Non-Comparative Data
rules.verbatim_quote: adequately prespecified adaptations based on non-comparative data have no effect or a limited effect on the Type I error probability. This makes them an attractive choice in many settings, particularly when uncertainty about event probabilities or endpoint variability is high. However, there is the potential for limited Type I error probability inflation in trials incorporating hypothesis tests of non-inferiority or equivalence. Sponsors should evaluate the extent of inflation in these scenarios.

rules.rule_text: Statistical methods for adaptive designs based on comparative data must take into account the adaptive trial design to ensure Type I error probability is controlled; performing analyses at the conventional 0.025 significance level without adjustment is not acceptable when comparative interim analyses are used for adaptation.
rules.source_section: Section V — Adaptive Designs Based on Comparative Data
rules.verbatim_quote: statistical methods should take into account the adaptive trial design. Second, when adaptations are based on comparative interim analyses, additional steps are critical to ensure appropriate trial conduct.

rules.rule_text: Group sequential designs must include prospectively planned interim analyses with prespecified stopping criteria; performing each hypothesis test at the conventional 0.025 significance level without alpha adjustment inflates Type I error probability and is not acceptable.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Group Sequential Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: Performing each of the multiple statistical hypothesis tests for efficacy in a group sequential trial at the conventional .025 one-sided significance level would therefore inflate the Type I error probability and, therefore, increase the chance of erroneous conclusions. A variety of methods exist to determine appropriate stopping boundaries for the interim and final analyses such that the Type I error probability is appropriately controlled.

rules.rule_text: If a trial continues despite meeting prespecified binding futility stopping rules, the Agency will likely consider that trial to have failed to provide evidence of efficacy regardless of the outcome at the final analysis.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Group Sequential Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: Therefore, if a trial continues despite meeting prespecified binding futility rules, the Agency will likely consider that trial to have failed to provide evidence of efficacy, regardless of the outcome at the final analysis.
rules.exceptions: Nonbinding futility guidelines do not create this constraint

rules.rule_text: For group sequential designs, an approach for calculating estimates and confidence intervals that appropriately accounts for the group sequential design must be prospectively planned and used for reporting results.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Group Sequential Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: an approach for calculating estimates and confidence intervals that appropriately accounts for the group sequential design should be prospectively planned and used for reporting results.

rules.rule_text: For sample size adaptations based on comparative interim results (unblinded sample size re-estimation), one of the variety of available methods to appropriately control the Type I error probability must be used; indiscriminate modification of sample size without proper adjustment can more than double the Type I error probability.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Adaptations to the Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: Indiscriminately modifying the sample size of a trial without proper adjustment can inflate the Type I error probability. Consider a design with one interim analysis at which the interim estimate of treatment effect is used to modify the final sample size. If one carries out a hypothesis test at the end of the trial at the conventional .025 significance level, the Type I error probability can be more than doubled.

rules.rule_text: Sample size adaptations involving treatment assignment information require additional steps to maintain trial integrity beyond those required for non-comparative pooled adaptations.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Adaptations to the Sample Size
rules.verbatim_quote: unlike adaptations based on non-comparative pooled interim estimates of nuisance parameters (section IV.), these adaptations involve treatment assignment information and, therefore, require additional steps to maintain trial integrity (section VII.).

rules.rule_text: For adaptive enrichment designs, the proposed adaptive modifications to the patient population must be motivated by results from prior trials and/or strong biologic plausibility; threshold(s) used to define subpopulations must be appropriately justified; and if identification of the targeted subpopulation depends on an in vitro diagnostic device, that device must have adequate performance characteristics.
rules.source_section: Section V.C — Adaptations to the Patient Population (Adaptive Enrichment)
rules.verbatim_quote: In the case of an adaptive enrichment design, the proposed adaptive modifications to the patient population should be motivated by results from previous (e.g., early-phase) trials and/or strong biologic plausibility that the benefit-risk profile will be most favorable in a particular subpopulation. Second, if the baseline characteristic that is thought to modify the treatment effect is not binary in nature, any threshold or thresholds used to define subpopulations should be appropriately justified. Third, the identification of the targeted subpopulation may depend on the use of an in vitro diagnostic device. In this scenario, the diagnostic device should have adequate performance characteristics.
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Enrichment Strategies for Clinical Trials (March 2019)
FDA Guidance: In Vitro Companion Diagnostic Devices (August 2014)

rules.rule_text: For trials intended to provide substantial evidence of effectiveness, statistical hypothesis testing methods must account for adaptive selection of treatment arms from among multiple doses evaluated in the trial, as well as any additional adaptive modifications.
rules.source_section: Section V.D — Adaptations to Treatment Arm Selection
rules.verbatim_quote: For trials intended to provide substantial evidence of effectiveness, statistical hypothesis testing methods should account for the adaptive selection of a best dose or doses from among the multiple doses evaluated in the trial, as well as any additional adaptive modifications, such as the potential to stop the trial early or to modify future sample sizes.

rules.rule_text: Platform trials that involve investigational agents from more than one sponsor, may be conducted for an unstated length of time, and often involve complex adaptations should involve extensive discussion with FDA.
rules.source_section: Section V.D — Adaptations to Treatment Arm Selection
rules.verbatim_quote: Because these trials may involve investigational agents from more than one sponsor, may be conducted for an unstated length of time, and often involve complex adaptations, they should generally involve extensive discussion with FDA.

rules.rule_text: For adaptive endpoint selection designs, the adaptation rule must be prespecified; statistical hypothesis testing methods must account for the adaptive endpoint selection; and early discussion with the FDA review division is recommended.
rules.source_section: Section V.F — Adaptations to Endpoint Selection
rules.verbatim_quote: As with other adaptive designs, the adaptation rule should be prespecified, and statistical hypothesis testing methods should account for the adaptive endpoint selection. Because endpoint selection involves important clinical considerations, early discussion with the FDA review division is recommended when such designs are being considered.

rules.rule_text: For complex adaptive designs combining multiple adaptive features, clinical trial simulations will often be critical to evaluate the trial design and estimate operating characteristics.
rules.source_section: Section V.G — Adaptations to Multiple Design Features
rules.verbatim_quote: It may be particularly difficult to estimate Type I error probability and other operating characteristics for designs that incorporate multiple adaptive features. Clinical trial simulations (section VI.A.) will often be critical to evaluate the trial design.

rules.rule_text: For complex adaptive designs where theoretical distributions are not available, simulations must be used to evaluate trial operating characteristics and demonstrate they meet desired levels; simulations should use at least 100,000 iterations per scenario to ensure a 95% confidence interval for Type I error probability of approximately ±0.1%.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A — Simulations in Adaptive Design Planning
rules.verbatim_quote: Using 100,000 iterations per scenario, for instance, ensures a 95% confidence interval for estimated Type I error probability with a width of approximately ± 0.1%, which would be sufficient for most cases.
rules.exceptions: Fewer iterations may suffice if a fine grid of scenarios is explored and all estimated Type I error probabilities are below the desired level

rules.rule_text: For Bayesian adaptive designs using Bayesian inference with informative prior distributions, FDA's draft guidance on Interacting with the FDA on Complex Innovative Clinical Trial Designs applies and relevant information must be submitted to FDA to facilitate review.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B — Bayesian Adaptive Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA's draft guidance for industry Interacting with the FDA on Complex Innovative Clinical Trial Designs for Drugs and Biological Products (September 2019) provides recommendations on what information should be submitted to FDA to facilitate the review of trial design proposals that use Bayesian inference.
cross_references: FDA Draft Guidance: Interacting with the FDA on Complex Innovative Clinical Trial Designs (September 2019)

rules.rule_text: In time-to-event settings, any use of surrogate or intermediate outcome information at interim analyses must be appropriately accounted for in the analysis; using such information without appropriate statistical analysis techniques can increase the chance of an erroneous conclusion of effectiveness.
rules.source_section: Section VI.D — Adaptations Based on a Potential Surrogate or Intermediate Endpoint
rules.verbatim_quote: In adaptive design trials with time-to-event or longitudinal outcomes, using surrogate or intermediate outcome information at the interim analysis can increase the chance of an erroneous conclusion of effectiveness unless appropriate statistical analysis techniques are used.

rules.rule_text: Secondary endpoint analyses must be appropriately adjusted for adaptive design features; without such adjustment, Type I error probability inflation and biased estimation can apply to any endpoint correlated with the primary endpoint.
rules.source_section: Section VI.E — Secondary Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Without such adjustment, appropriate caution should be applied in interpreting secondary endpoint results.
rules.exceptions: For group sequential approaches, methods exist to adjust secondary endpoint analyses

rules.rule_text: Trials with early stopping for efficacy must still collect sufficient safety data to allow a reliable benefit-risk evaluation and to inform labeling; the size of the safety database must be taken into account when planning the number, timing, and stopping boundaries of interim analyses.
rules.source_section: Section VI.F — Safety Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Trials with early stopping for strong evidence of effectiveness still need to collect sufficient safety data to allow for a reliable benefit-risk evaluation of the investigational drug and to inform labeling. For this reason, the size of a safety database should be taken into account when planning the number, timing, and stopping boundaries of interim analyses.

rules.rule_text: In early-phase dose-escalation adaptive trials, the speed of escalation must be carefully considered because rapidly escalating cohorts could lead to subjects receiving unsafe high doses, particularly when there is possibility of delayed-onset adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section VI.G — Adaptive Design in Early-Phase Exploratory Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: Adaptation rules that allow for successive cohorts of subjects to receive quickly escalating doses could lead to subjects receiving unsafe high doses that would have been avoided by a design with more gradual dose-escalation. This is particularly true when there is a possibility for serious adverse events with a delayed onset of action of the investigational drug. For this reason, the speed of escalation should be considered in choosing a specific adaptation rule in an adaptive dose-escalation trial.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors are strongly discouraged from implementing unplanned design changes based on comparative interim results without first meeting with FDA to discuss the changes, provided patient safety is not compromised.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H — Unplanned Design Changes Based on Comparative Interim Results
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors are strongly discouraged from implementing such changes without first meeting with FDA to discuss the changes being considered, provided patient safety is not compromised.
rules.exceptions: Emergency patient safety actions do not require prior FDA discussion

rules.rule_text: A clinical trial with an adaptive design must include rigorous planning, careful implementation, and comprehensive documentation of approaches taken to maintain confidentiality of comparative interim results and to preserve trial integrity.
rules.source_section: Section VII — Maintaining Trial Integrity
rules.verbatim_quote: a clinical trial with an adaptive design should include rigorous planning, careful implementation, and comprehensive documentation of approaches taken to maintain confidentiality of comparative interim results and to preserve trial integrity.

rules.rule_text: The committee tasked with making adaptation recommendations must include members with the proper expertise, including a statistician or statisticians knowledgeable about the adaptation methodology, the data monitoring plan, and the decision rules.
rules.source_section: Section VII — Maintaining Trial Integrity
rules.verbatim_quote: Regardless of the approach chosen, the committee tasked with making adaptation recommendations should have members with the proper expertise, including a statistician or statisticians who are knowledgeable about the adaptation methodology, the data monitoring plan, and the decision rules.
cross_references: FDA Guidance: Establishment and Operation of Clinical Trial Data Monitoring Committees (March 2006)

rules.rule_text: Personnel responsible for preparing and reporting interim analysis results to the DMC or adaptation committee must be physically and logistically separated from the personnel tasked with managing and conducting the trial.
rules.source_section: Section VII — Maintaining Trial Integrity
rules.verbatim_quote: Safeguards should be in place to ensure that the persons responsible for preparing and reporting interim analysis results to the DMC or the adaptation committee are physically and logistically separated from the personnel tasked with managing and conducting the trial.

rules.rule_text: Documentation submitted to FDA prior to initiation of an adaptive design trial must include: a rationale for the selected design; a detailed description of the adaptation plan; information on roles of the bodies responsible for implementing the adaptive design; prespecification of statistical methods; evaluation and discussion of design operating characteristics including Type I error probability, power, expected/minimum/maximum sample size, bias of treatment effect estimates, and coverage of confidence intervals; and a comprehensive written data access plan.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.B — Documentation Prior to Conducting an Adaptive Trial
rules.verbatim_quote: A detailed description of the adaptation plan, including the anticipated number and timing of interim analyses, the specific aspects of the design that may be modified, and the rule that will be used to make adaptation decisions. [...] Evaluation and discussion of the design operating characteristics, which should typically include Type I error probability; power; expected, minimum, and maximum sample size; bias of treatment effect estimates; and coverage of confidence intervals.

rules.rule_text: When simulations are the primary or sole technique for evaluating trial operating characteristics, a detailed simulation report must be submitted including: an overall description of the trial design; example trials; parameter configurations with justification; number of iterations per scenario with rationale; simulation results; simulation code (including random seeds); and an overall summary.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.B — Documentation Prior to Conducting an Adaptive Trial
rules.verbatim_quote: In cases where simulations are the primary or sole technique for evaluating trial operating characteristics as defined above, a detailed simulation report should be submitted, including: An overall description of the trial design. [...] Simulation code. Because FDA reviewers will need to verify simulation studies used to evaluate trial operating characteristics, it is important to document the software package used for simulations and, if custom software was used, to provide the code used for the simulations. [...] It is also helpful to provide code written in widely-used statistical programming languages.

rules.rule_text: For late-phase adaptive clinical trials, sponsors should obtain formal FDA feedback at an End-of-Phase-2 meeting; the submission of Special Protocol Assessments for trials with complex adaptive designs is recommended only if there has been extensive previous discussion between FDA and the sponsor.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.A — Interactions With FDA
rules.verbatim_quote: Depending on the preexisting knowledge regarding the drug and its intended use, and the nature of the adaptive features, an EOP2 meeting may be the appropriate setting for a sponsor to obtain FDA feedback. [...] Given the timelines (45-day responses) and commitments involved with special protocol assessments (SPAs), we recommend the submission of SPAs for trials with complex adaptive designs only if there has been extensive previous discussion between FDA and the sponsor regarding the proposed trial and design.

rules.rule_text: A marketing application that relies on an adaptive design trial must include: all prospective plans and relevant committee charters; information on compliance with the adaptation rule and data access plan; records of deliberations for any interim discussions; results of interim analyses used for adaptation decisions; and appropriate reporting of the adaptive design and trial results in section 14 of the proposed package insert.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.C — Evaluating and Reporting a Completed Trial
rules.verbatim_quote: A marketing application to FDA that relies on a trial with an adaptive design should include sufficient information and documentation to allow FDA to thoroughly review the results. In particular, in addition to the typical content of an NDA or a BLA, the application should include the following: All prospective plans, any relevant committee charters [...] Information on compliance with the planned adaptation rule and compliance with the procedures outlined in the data access plan to maintain trial integrity. Records of deliberations and participants for any interim discussions by any committees [...] Results of the interim analysis or analyses used for the adaptation decisions. Appropriate reporting of the adaptive design and trial results in section 14 of the proposed package insert.

rules.rule_text: For the Lan-DeMets alpha-spending approach, sponsors must put in place additional safeguards including a targeted number of interim analyses and an approximate schedule for their occurrence, as well as a decision framework for changing the number or timing of analyses after the trial has begun; this decision framework must be based on information statistically independent of the estimated treatment effect.
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Group Sequential Designs
rules.verbatim_quote: Because of this potential issue with the Lan-DeMets alpha-spending approach, sponsors should put in place additional safeguards such as a targeted number of interim analyses and an approximate schedule for their occurrence, as well as a decision framework for changing the number or timing of analyses after the trial has begun. The decision framework should be based on information that is statistically independent of the estimated treatment effect (e.g., enrollment rate or scheduling logistics).

rules.rule_text: A comprehensive written data access plan must be developed and documented defining how trial integrity will be maintained, specifying: personnel who will perform interim analyses; personnel who will have access to interim results; how access will be controlled; how adaptive decisions will be made; and what type of information will be disseminated following adaptive decisions.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.B — Documentation Prior to Conducting an Adaptive Trial
rules.verbatim_quote: A comprehensive written data access plan defining how trial integrity will be maintained in the presence of the planned adaptations. This documentation should include information regarding: (1) the personnel who will perform the interim analyses; (2) the personnel who will have access to interim results; (3) how that access will be controlled; (4) how adaptive decisions will be made; and (5) what type of information will be disseminated following adaptive decisions, and to whom it will be disseminated.

rules.rule_text: Treatment effect estimates in the marketing application must adequately take the adaptive design into account; if naive estimates such as unadjusted sample means are used, the extent of bias must be evaluated and estimates must be presented with appropriate cautions regarding their interpretation.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.C — Evaluating and Reporting a Completed Trial
rules.verbatim_quote: treatment effect estimates should adequately take the design into account, or if naive estimates such as unadjusted sample means are used, the extent of bias should be evaluated, and estimates should be presented with appropriate cautions regarding their interpretation.
